text.title: pytest-django Test Patterns
text.summary: Unit tests, API integration tests, parametrize, and mocking external services.

rule.statement: Mark test classes or functions with @pytest.mark.django_db only when DB access is required. Pure logic tests must not touch the DB.
rule.rationale: DB setup costs time; pure logic tests run much faster without it. --strict-markers makes missing markers a hard error.

rule.statement: Structure each test: Arrange (set up data) → Act (call code under test) → Assert (verify observable outcome). One logical assertion group per test.
rule.rationale: Tests with multiple unrelated assertions fail for multiple reasons simultaneously, making the root cause ambiguous.

rule.statement: Use APIClient.force_authenticate instead of issuing real login requests in integration tests — much faster and tests the endpoint, not the auth flow.
rule.rationale: Real login calls include password hashing and token generation — 100-1000ms per test. force_authenticate bypasses all of that.

rule.statement: Use @pytest.mark.parametrize for boundary and equivalence-class testing where the same logic runs against multiple inputs.
rule.rationale: Replaces repetitive test functions with a single declaration; failures show exactly which parameter set failed.

rule.statement: Mock at the I/O boundary (HTTP, mail, payment), test the rest with real DB and real serialization. Over-mocking produces tests that prove nothing.
rule.rationale: Mocking internal methods tests implementation, not behavior. Tests that mock the wrong import path pass silently with zero real coverage.

rule.statement: Use transactional_db (not db) when the code under test calls transaction.on_commit — on_commit callbacks never fire inside a rolled-back transaction.
rule.rationale: db wraps each test in a savepoint that never commits; on_commit handlers that send emails or dispatch tasks are silently skipped.
